Name: adaptive_geometric_precision_routing
Title: Dynamic Precision Routing Through Geometric Diagnostics for Resource-Efficient Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing real-time geometric diagnostics of activation covariances, we can dynamically optimize numerical precision in machine learning models, leading to improved runtime efficiency and robustness against distribution shifts without the need for retraining.
Related Work: Current literature emphasizes static or heuristic-based precision allocation methods in mixed-precision inference, such as Spectral Latent Steering and Spectral Quantization Routing. However, these methods lack an adaptive mechanism that responds to real-time changes in the model's operational environment. Our proposal distinctly integrates dynamic geometric diagnostics with a routing framework that adapts precision allocation based on observed activation properties, thus introducing a novel, proactive approach to resource management in ML.
Abstract: In the pursuit of efficient machine learning deployment, the dynamic allocation of computational resources remains a critical challenge. We propose a novel framework that integrates real-time geometric diagnostics of activation covariances to drive adaptive precision routing in neural networks. By dynamically assessing the condition number and spectral decay of hidden state activations, our approach selectively allocates numerical precision based on the observed geometry of the operational subspace. This training-free mechanism not only reduces computational overhead but also enhances robustness against distributional shifts. We establish theoretical grounding through PAC-Bayes bounds linking geometric diagnostics to generalization stability under quantization noise. Our empirical evaluation, conducted on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks, demonstrates a superior throughput-accuracy trade-off compared to existing static mixed-precision baselines, paving the way for scalable and resource-efficient machine learning applications.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline mixed-precision model for comparative analysis.
2. Develop the dynamic geometric diagnostic module to compute real-time covariances and spectral properties of activations.
3. Conduct experiments varying precision thresholds based on geometric diagnostics to assess throughput and accuracy across multiple benchmarks.
4. Evaluate performance in scenarios with distributional shifts to quantify robustness improvements.
Metrics will include throughput (inferences per second), accuracy (F1 score, accuracy metrics), and computational overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Dependence on accurate geometric diagnostics; potential inaccuracies in covariance estimation could affect precision routing efficacy.
Limited by the complexity of real-time computation; the overhead of diagnostic calculations may offset benefits in low-computation scenarios.
The generalizability of the proposed method across diverse architectures and datasets needs thorough empirical validation.
